attractions.name: Serpas True Food
attractions.description: Serpas True Food was a celebrated new-American restaurant by Chef Scott Serpas, featuring a menu influenced by his Louisiana roots. Housed in a historic 1900s cotton storage facility in the Old Fourth Ward, the restaurant combined industrial-chic aesthetics with soulful, approachable cuisine. It gained national acclaim, including being named one of GQ's 10 Best New Restaurants in America shortly after its 2009 opening. Atlanta Journal-Constitution, OpenTable

attractions.name: Staplehouse (Staplehouse)
attractions.description: Staplehouse is a charming and casual neighborhood restaurant located in Atlanta's historic Old Fourth Ward. It offers an ever-rotating seasonal menu featuring pizzas, sandwiches, and pastries made with local ingredients. Notably, the restaurant operates as a for-profit subsidiary of the Giving Kitchen, a nonprofit that supports food service workers in need (Staplehouse, Explore Georgia).
attractions.hours: Monday: Closed, Tuesday: Closed, Wednesday: Closed, Thursday: 12:00 PM - 9:00 PM, Friday: 12:00 PM - 10:00 PM, Saturday: 12:00 PM - 10:00 PM, Sunday: 12:00 PM - 9:00 PM (Staplehouse)

attractions.tips: Arrive early as seating is walk-in only and can fill up quickly (Staplehouse). Take advantage of the dog-friendly patio for a relaxed outdoor dining experience (Atly). Look for free street parking on Edgewood Avenue or Howell Street before opting for nearby paid valet (Staplehouse).

attractions.name: Sweet Auburn Historic District
attractions.description: The Sweet Auburn Historic District is a historic African-American neighborhood centered along Auburn Avenue in Atlanta, Georgia. Coined the "richest Negro street in the world" by John Wesley Dobbs, it served as the commercial and cultural heart of black Atlanta during the Jim Crow era and is the birthplace of Martin Luther King Jr. Today, it is a National Historic Landmark preserving iconic churches, businesses, and museums pivotal to the Civil Rights Movement. (Explore Georgia, Wikipedia, Discover Atlanta)
attractions.hours: MLK National Historical Park: Daily 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM (NPS); Sweet Auburn Curb Market: Mon–Sat 8:00 AM – 8:00 PM, Closed Sun (Municipal Market); APEX Museum: Tue–Sat 11:00 AM – 3:00 PM, Closed Sun–Mon (APEX Museum)
attractions.admission: District & Martin Luther King Jr. National Historical Park: Free (FORTH Atlanta), APEX Museum: Adults $12.00, Seniors/Students/Veterans $8.00, Children (4 & under) Free (APEX Museum)
highlights: ['Martin Luther King Jr. Birth Home', 'Historic Ebenezer Baptist Church', 'The King Center (Tomb of Dr. and Mrs. King)', 'Sweet Auburn Curb Market (Municipal Market)', 'APEX Museum (African American Panoramic Experience)', "John Lewis 'Hero' Mural and Civil Rights Walk of Fame"]
attractions.tips: Take the Atlanta Streetcar for easy travel between the district and downtown. Arrive early at the MLK Visitor Center to book Birth Home tours in person, as they are first-come, first-served and fill up quickly. Wear comfortable walking shoes to explore the murals and historic architecture along Auburn Avenue. (The King Center, Discover Atlanta)
attractions.nearby: National Center for Civil and Human Rights, Georgia Aquarium, World of Coca-Cola, Centennial Olympic Park, Historic Oakland Cemetery (Discover Atlanta, Expedia)

attractions.name: Sweet Auburn Historic District / Martin Luther King, Jr. National Historical Park
attractions.category: National Historical Park, Historic District, Landmark, Museum
attractions.description: Auburn Avenue is the heart of the Sweet Auburn Historic District, once celebrated as the "richest Negro street in the world" and the commercial center of Black life in Atlanta. It is the birthplace of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. and serves as a vital landmark of the American Civil Rights Movement, preserved today as part of a National Historical Park. Visitors can explore the neighborhood where Dr. King lived, worked, and preached, connecting with his enduring legacy of nonviolence and social justice. (New Georgia Encyclopedia, National Park Service)
attractions.hours: Monday: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Tuesday: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Wednesday: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Thursday: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Friday: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Saturday: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Sunday: 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM (National Park Service)
attractions.days_open: Daily (Closed on Thanksgiving Day, Christmas Day, and New Year's Day) (National Park Service)
attractions.admission: Free (No entrance fee or pass required) (National Park Service)
highlights: 'Martin Luther King, Jr. Birth Home: The restored Queen Anne-style house where Dr. King was born in 1929. ([National Park Service)', 'Historic Ebenezer Baptist Church: The spiritual home where Dr. King co-pastored with his father. (National Park Service)', 'The King Center: Houses the tombs of Dr. King and Coretta Scott King, along with an eternal flame and reflecting pool. (The King Center)', 'International Civil Rights Walk of Fame: A sidewalk memorial featuring the footprints of legendary civil rights activists. (Discover Atlanta)', 'Sweet Auburn Curb Market: A historic 1918 indoor market offering diverse local food, produce, and artisan goods. (Discover Atlanta)', 'APEX Museum: A museum dedicated to chronicling the African American experience through history and art. (Discover Atlanta)']
attractions.tips: Begin your visit at the Temporary Visitor Center (currently at Fire Station No. 6) to pick up maps and sign up for ranger-led tours. Arrive early, as tours of the Birth Home are popular and limited. Consider using the Atlanta Streetcar for convenient access to and from Downtown. Wear comfortable walking shoes, as most major sites are within a short walking distance of each other. (National Park Service, Atlanta Parent)
attractions.nearby: Historic Oakland Cemetery, Atlanta BeltLine Eastside Trail, Krog Street Market, Georgia State University, and Centennial Olympic Park. (Tripadvisor, Discover Atlanta)

attractions.name: The Martin Luther King, Jr. Center for Nonviolent Social Change (The King Center)
attractions.description: Established in 1968 by Mrs. Coretta Scott King, The King Center is a global destination and community institution dedicated to the legacy of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. It serves as a resource center for nonviolent social change and is the final resting place of Dr. and Mrs. King, attracting nearly a million visitors annually. The campus features exhibits, monuments, and gardens that inspire visitors with Dr. King’s philosophy of nonviolence. The King Center, ATL Airport District
attractions.hours: Sunday – Saturday: 10:00 am – 5:00 pm (Closed on Thanksgiving Day, Christmas Eve, Christmas Day, and New Year’s Day) The King Center
attractions.days_open: 7 days a week The King Center
attractions.admission: Free (Admission and Parking are FREE) The King Center, NPS
highlights: 'Dr. & Mrs. King’s Crypt (situated in the middle of a Reflecting Pool) [The King Center', 'Freedom Hall (featuring exhibits on Dr. King, Mrs. King, Mahatma Gandhi, and Rosa Parks) The King Center', 'The Eternal Flame (symbolizing the continuing effort for a world of justice and peace) The King Center', 'Coretta Scott King Peace and Meditation Garden The King Center', 'Freedom Walkway and Yolanda D. King Theatre for the Performing Arts The King Center', "The 'Behold' Monument and Civil Rights Walk of Fame (adjacent to the center) AMLI Blog"]
attractions.tips: Tours of Dr. King’s Birth Home are conducted by the National Park Service on a first-come, first-served basis; registrations must be made in person at the Visitor Center on the day of the tour. It is recommended to arrive early as tours fill up quickly, especially on weekends and holidays. For easier access, consider using the Atlanta Streetcar or MARTA bus routes #3 and #99. The King Center

attractions.name: East Atlanta Village (EAV)
attractions.description: East Atlanta Village (EAV) is a walkable, edgy neighborhood southeast of downtown Atlanta known for its thriving independent character and alternative culture. The district is home to some of the city's most talked-about music venues, diverse international cuisine, and a dense collection of locally-owned shops and street art. Discover Atlanta, CityPASS

attractions.name: Virginia-Highland Neighborhood (VaHi)
attractions.description: Virginia-Highland (often called "VaHi") is a historic Atlanta neighborhood founded in the early 20th century as a streetcar suburb. Known for its walkable streets lined with Craftsman bungalows and Tudor cottages, it serves as a premier destination for shopping, dining, and nightlife centered around its eponymous intersection.

attractions.admission: Free (Public neighborhood with various free community parks and trails; individual businesses and festivals may have costs)

attractions.tips: Park once and explore on foot to fully experience the neighborhood's walkability; check out the Sunday farmers market at the elementary school; visit the unique shops on North Highland Ave for local gifts.

attractions.name: Zesto Atlanta (East Atlanta)
attractions.description: Zesto Atlanta is an iconic local institution that has been serving the Atlanta area since 1949. Originally started as a soft-serve ice cream stand, it expanded into a full drive-in menu featuring legendary burgers, footlong chili dogs, and fried chicken. Still family-owned, it remains a beloved neighborhood staple known for its retro atmosphere and classic American comfort food (Official Website).

attractions.admission: Free (Official Website)

attractions.tips: Don't miss the 'Chubby Decker' burger or the 'Nut Brown Crown' dipped cone for the full experience. The East Atlanta location features a convenient drive-thru and a small parking lot for those dining in (Yelp). Renowned for its "South on a Plate" flavors and historic significance, the restaurant served as a vital meeting spot for Civil Rights leaders, including Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., and continues to be a cherished culinary landmark today.
